summary: The Gemara debates whether priests wore sacred or non-sacred garments during the daily lottery, resolving that non-lottery-winners shed their sacred garments. Abaye proves from this that the Chamber of Hewn Stone (Lishkat HaGazit) straddled the boundary between sacred and non-sacred Temple precincts. The page then presents the Mishna listing the thirteen tasks assigned by the second daily lottery, followed by Ben Azzai's tradition about the correct order for carrying the tamid's limbs to the altar.

glossary.en: Lottery; the daily priestly lottery determining who performs which Temple service.
Chamber of Hewn Stone; the seat of the Sanhedrin, located partly in sacred and partly in non-sacred Temple precincts.
East; the eastern precinct of the Temple where daily service began at dawn.

rashiTranslations.en: Whether the lottery priests wore sacred or non-sacred garments is the question Rav Sheshet answers.
The proof from the baraita supports Rav Sheshet's position that they wore sacred garments.
The other view (Rav Nachman) resolves the difficulty differently without Rav Sheshet's assumption.
Rav Sheshet's own baraita source - the headdress (mitznefet) proves priests wore sacred garments.
The Gemara rejects: there is a non-sacred mitre, so the headdress doesn't prove sacred garments.
Abaye introduces his deduction about the Chamber of Hewn Stone from the garment baraita.
Abaye continues: if the chamber were entirely outside sacred precincts, the proof fails.
And if the chamber had only one doorway into the sacred area, the calculation still fails.
Mishna: The second lottery - thirteen tasks divided among thirteen priests for the morning tamid.
How the limbs were distributed: head with right leg, tail with left leg, etc.
Ben Azzai's alternative tradition: limbs carried in a different order before Rabbi Akiva.
Gemara asks: did the lottery assign all tasks together or separate the incense from other tasks?
The koliar is a round object from which the count begins.
The lottery count begins from the koliar as explained at the start of the chapter.
As R. Shmuel bar Yehuda teaches.
Cited later in chapter three (35b).
Mothers of kohanim would make for their sons.
From their own funds, vestment-like garments.
To display the beauty of their wealth and fine craftsmanship.
How greatly they would beautify the mitzvah - as the Mishna states.
If his mother made him a tunic, he may wear it for private avodah.
Sitting is prohibited in the Temple Courtyard.
As it says (Deut. 18) 'to stand and minister.'
Except for kings of the House of David.
For whom we find sitting, as it says.
(2 Sam. 7) 'And King David went in and sat before God.'
The verse 'who am I and what is my house' is cited.
But we need 'in the House of God let us walk together.'
Showing the lottery must occur in the House of God.
Mishna: who slaughters the tamid offering.
The head and right leg to one kohen.
Both forelegs to a second kohen; the tail.
The tail and left leg to a third kohen.
The chest - the fat membrane that faces the ground.
It is cut from both sides.
Without the rib ends; the throat area.
The neck with windpipe, liver, heart, chest, and throat.
The chest and throat to the fourth kohen.
The two flanks to the fifth; the innards.
To the sixth; the fine flour.
A tenth-ephah for the tamid meal-offering; the griddle-cakes (chavittin).
Half a tenth-ephah of the KG offered daily.
As it says (Lev. 6) 'half in the morning.'
'And half in the evening.'
Adjacent to 'on a griddle with oil shall it be made.'
Hence called chavittin; the wine - three log.
Libation wine for the tamid lamb; thirteen.
Kohanim win the lottery; this [first] lottery.
Thirteen services are enumerated here.
As the animal walked in life, the limbs are carried; the Gemara explains.
Gemara: one lottery for all services.
Such as who slaughters; once a winner is found.
The winner of slaughter does not re-draw.
For the remaining 12 services.
The kohanim to his right follow in the sequence of our Mishna.
Second gets blood sprinkling, third gets inner altar ash.
Fourth gets the menorah, and so on.
Or perhaps for each service separately.
They would again extend fingers for a new count.
And the winner takes that service.
No [one lottery for all services].

display.whats: Whether priests wore sacred garments during the lottery - and Abaye's geometric proof that the Chamber of Hewn Stone straddled the sacred/non-sacred boundary.
display.title: A garment dispute turns into an architectural proof: pinpointing where the Chamber of Hewn Stone stood
display.hint: The garments the priests wore reveal the precise architectural layout of the Temple precinct.

learning.learnerQuestion: Does it matter, halachically, exactly which patch of ground the Sanhedrin's Chamber of Hewn Stone stood on?
learning.coreTension: An architectural detail like a building's exact footprint sounds like antiquarian trivia, yet the Gemara builds a full deductive proof to pin it down, which only makes sense if the boundary carries real legal weight rather than being incidental description.
learning.coreMove: The geography question is downstream of the garments question. Sacred precincts require sacred garments; if the chamber where the lottery convened touches that precinct, the priests' attire while inside it needs explaining. Abaye reasons from the same baraita Rav Sheshet cited: the only way the details fit is if the Chamber of Hewn Stone has doorways opening into both the sacred and the non-sacred zones, meaning the building itself straddles the boundary.
learning.resolution: The Chamber of Hewn Stone straddled the sacred/non-sacred boundary, with doorways opening into both zones. This is established as architectural fact, but the Gemara does not spell out every further halachic consequence that follows from the boundary's exact location.

takeaway.text: A precise geographic boundary can carry real halachic weight whenever garment, entry, or conduct rules are zone-dependent; the boundary is not merely descriptive.

learning.ahaMoment: A debate that started as 'what did the priests wear' ends as a geometric proof about a building's footprint, because in a Temple where dress is governed by zone, pinning down the zone pins down the dress requirement too.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may expect the Gemara to state outright why the chamber's location matters practically. It does not generalize beyond the proof itself: it establishes where the boundary runs, using the garments dispute as its evidence, without separately stating every downstream consequence of that boundary.
learning.memoryAnchor: Imagine the Chamber of Hewn Stone as a building split exactly down the middle by the Temple wall, one half sacred, one half not, proven from a dispute about priestly headdresses.

argumentFlow.label: Lottery garments?
argumentFlow.text: Did the priests who won the lottery wear sacred or non-sacred garments during the lottery ceremony?

argumentFlow.label: Rav Sheshet: non-winners shed sacred garments
argumentFlow.text: Rav Sheshet: the mishna describes non-lottery-winners, who shed their sacred garments after failing to win.

argumentFlow.label: Baraita: headdress proof
argumentFlow.text: Rav Sheshet proves priests wore sacred garments: a baraita mentions the mitznefet (priestly headdress) being removed - only relevant if they wore full sacred garb.

argumentFlow.label: Non-sacred mitre exists
argumentFlow.text: Rejected: there is a non-sacred headdress (mitre), so the mitznefet mention does not prove sacred garments.

argumentFlow.label: Abaye: Chamber of Hewn Stone location
argumentFlow.text: Abaye derives from the same baraita that the Chamber of Hewn Stone had doorways in both sacred and non-sacred precincts, straddling the border.

theological: precise Temple geography as halachic evidence

misconceptions.misconception: The mishna describes the lottery winners shedding their sacred garments.
misconceptions.correction: Rav Sheshet reads the mishna as describing the non-winners, who shed their sacred garments after failing to win a task.

misconceptions.misconception: The Chamber of Hewn Stone (Lishkat HaGazit) sat entirely outside the sacred precincts.
misconceptions.correction: Abaye's proof from the garment baraita establishes that it straddled the sacred/non-sacred boundary, with doorways opening on both sides.

visualizableElements.label: Chamber of Hewn Stone
visualizableElements.description: Building straddling the sacred/non-sacred boundary with doors opening in both directions

visualizableElements.label: Sacred vs non-sacred garments
visualizableElements.description: Priests in sacred headdresses inside the chamber vs common attire outside

quizSeeds.question: According to Rav Sheshet, which priests does the mishna describe removing their garments after the lottery?
quizSeeds.answer: The non-winners who had worn sacred garments and now removed them after failing to win a task.

quizSeeds.question: What architectural fact does Abaye deduce from the lottery garment baraita?
quizSeeds.answer: That the Chamber of Hewn Stone straddled the sacred/non-sacred boundary, with doorways opening into both areas.

sugyot.finalRuling: The lottery was conducted in the Chamber of Hewn Stone, which sat at the sacred-to-non-sacred boundary. The practical question of whether failures to follow garment protocol invalidate service remains unresolved.

display.whats: The Mishna lists the thirteen tasks assigned by the second daily lottery. Ben Azzai transmits a different tradition for the order in which the tamid's limbs were carried up to the altar.
display.title: Thirteen fixed tasks, then a competing tradition: Ben Azzai's alternate order for the tamid's limbs
display.hint: The second lottery assigned 13 tasks covering the entire tamid service from slaughter through limb-carrying.

learning.learnerQuestion: If the mishna already lays out a fixed order for carrying the tamid's limbs, why does Ben Azzai introduce a competing sequence in Rabbi Yehoshua's name?
learning.coreTension: A precisely choreographed list, thirteen tasks, exact limb pairings, reads like settled law, yet a named tanna immediately offers a different sequence attributed to an earlier authority. That is a strange thing to transmit if the order were simply fixed fact with nothing left to debate.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara registers Ben Azzai's tradition as a real, attributed alternative, not a casual variant, and then opens its own structural question: was the lottery that assigned these thirteen tasks a single lottery, or were some tasks, like the incense, assigned separately? The sequence dispute and the lottery-structure question are connected: both probe how rigid versus how negotiated the Temple's daily choreography actually was.
learning.resolution: The mishna's sequence is followed as the operative practice for carrying the tamid's limbs. Ben Azzai's competing tradition, transmitted in Rabbi Yehoshua's name, remains a live point of debate that the Gemara carries forward rather than resolving here.

takeaway.text: An operative mishnaic sequence and a transmitted minority tradition can stand side by side in the text without the Gemara forcing an immediate resolution between them.

learning.ahaMoment: The thirteen lottery positions are not arbitrary; each maps to a distinct moment in the tamid service. Yet even a routine this precisely choreographed still carries an unresolved alternate tradition about its own order, showing the choreography was transmitted, and contested, not simply self-evident.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may expect the Gemara to settle here whether Ben Azzai's order reflects an actual historical change in Temple practice or just a different reading of the same source. The sugya does not decide this; it carries the question forward into the next daf.
learning.memoryAnchor: Thirteen lottery tasks, mishna's order operative; Ben Azzai (in Rabbi Yehoshua's name) transmits a different limb-carrying order, an open dispute, not yet resolved.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna: 2nd lottery - 13 tasks
argumentFlow.text: The second daily lottery assigned thirteen tasks for the morning tamid service, from slaughtering through carrying the limbs.

argumentFlow.label: Limb pairing
argumentFlow.text: The mishna specifies how the limbs were paired for carrying: head with right leg (one priest), tail with left leg (one priest), etc.

argumentFlow.label: Ben Azzai's alternate order
argumentFlow.text: Ben Azzai transmitted in Rabbi Yehoshua's name a different order for carrying the limbs to the altar.

argumentFlow.label: Single or separate lottery for incense?
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara opens: did the lottery assign all tasks together, or was incense a separate lottery?

narrative: dispute between tannaim on service sequence

misconceptions.misconception: The second lottery assigned only a few tasks.
misconceptions.correction: It assigned thirteen distinct roles covering the entire morning tamid service, from slaughter through carrying every limb.

misconceptions.misconception: Ben Azzai's different order is his own independent opinion.
misconceptions.correction: It is a received tradition, transmitted in the name of Rabbi Yehoshua, not Ben Azzai's own innovation.

visualizableElements.label: Thirteen lottery tasks
visualizableElements.description: Ordered list of 13 tamid tasks assigned by the second lottery

visualizableElements.label: Limb pairing
visualizableElements.description: Which limbs were carried together by one priest to the altar ramp

quizSeeds.question: How many tasks did the second daily lottery assign?
quizSeeds.answer: Thirteen tasks, covering the full tamid service from slaughter through carrying all the limbs.

quizSeeds.question: In whose name did Ben Azzai transmit his alternate limb-order tradition?
quizSeeds.answer: In the name of Rabbi Yehoshua.

sugyot.finalRuling: The mishna's sequence is the operative practice; Ben Azzai's tradition opens a debate continued on 25b.
